Past where the old trail splits. You'll know it by the smell
Near the foundation stones. The ones the settlers built over
Down in the low ground where the fog settles thick
Follow the creek until it goes underground. The entrance is just past that point
There's a cairn on the ridge where two valleys meet. Head north from there
You'll pass a stretch of dead ground first. Nothing growing, just bare earth. Keep going past that
The ground changes underfoot. Soft soil turns to packed stone. That's how you know you're close
Look for where the moss grows thickest on the rock face. There's a gap behind it
Up the switchback trail about an hour's walk. Steep near the top, watch your footing
The old road still has cart ruts if you know where to look. Follow those east
There's a hollow where the wind sounds different. Lower pitch, almost a moan. That's the place
Keep the river on your left. When you can't hear it anymore, you've gone too far
The locals call it the Scarred Hill. You'll see why when you get there: whole face is stripped bare
It's built into the cliff side. Easy to miss from below because the overhang hides the opening
The water there tastes like iron. Metallic and cold. Good landmark if nothing else
South of the standing stones, maybe half a day on foot. Faster if you cut through the ravine
There's a collapsed bridge over the gorge. The path down to the crossing is on the western bank
You'll see smoke sometimes, thin and white, rising from between the rocks. That's the hot springs marking the edge
The soil turns red in that area. Some kind of clay beneath the surface. Hard to miss once you're in it
Head toward the highest ground you can see from here. The cave system opens at its base, facing away from the wind
Behind the main hall, near where the old storage used to be
The south quarter, where most of the workshops are set up
Near the market square. You can't miss the spot, everyone passes through there
Around the back of the council building. Not many people go that way
By the outer wall, on the side facing the lower ground
The old quarter near the gate. Some of the original foundations are still visible
Down by the well. It's where everyone gathers when there's news
Just past the smithy, in the open area where they hold assemblies
Where the two footpaths cross. There's a flat stone there that the children sit on
Follow the dry streambed. It'll curve west and end at a rock shelf. The opening is underneath
The ground dips suddenly. A depression, almost bowl-shaped. Hard to see until you're standing at the edge
There's a ring of pale stones near the treeline. Natural formation, but people treat it like a boundary marker
About a half-day's walk, if you don't stop. The terrain isn't difficult, just long
The path narrows to single file between two boulders. Past that squeeze, the ground opens up again
Look for the tree with the split trunk. Lightning took it years ago. Turn left there
The creek forks where a large boulder sits in the channel. Take the left branch
There's a shelf of flat rock jutting out from the hillside. Good vantage point. You can see the whole valley from there
Where the pines give way to birch, the soil changes. That's the boundary of the old land
The ruins sit on a ledge about halfway up the slope. You can see the foundation walls from the valley floor on a clear day
You'll smell the sulfur before you see anything. The hot spring vents are unmistakable
Past the old mill foundation. The wheel is gone but the channel is still carved into the rock
The cave mouth faces north. It catches the cold wind, so dress warm before you go in
There's a series of shallow pools connected by trickles of water. Follow them upstream to the source
The terrain becomes rocky and uneven. Ankle-turning country. Watch your step after the tree line
A fallen tree bridges a gully. Cross it and you're in the right area. The bark is still solid enough to hold
The cliff face has horizontal bands of color. Dark grey, rust red, pale cream. Navigation by geology
Where the old stone fence ends. It runs for maybe a hundred paces and then just stops. Nobody knows who built it
There's a patch of ground that stays muddy even in dry weather. A spring feeds it from below
The clearing was made by fire, not by hand. You can tell from the charred stumps around the perimeter
South-facing slope, sheltered from the prevailing wind. That's why things grow there that don't grow elsewhere
The overhang creates a natural shelter. Dry ground under it even in heavy rain. People have been using it for ages
You'll cross three ridges. The place is in the valley beyond the third. Easier to count ridges than track distance
There's a section of the path where the rock is polished smooth. Water flows across it in the wet season
A stand of dead trees marks the area. They died all at once years ago. Something in the soil changed
The ravine is narrow enough to jump at one point. The edges are undercut, so don't linger near the rim
An old campfire ring, blackened stones in a circle, marks the usual rest point. The next stretch is harder
The slope faces west, so it catches the afternoon light. Warm and dry, good for growing but bad for water
Keep to the high ground. The low path floods after any rain, and the mud is the kind that takes your boots
There's a notch in the ridgeline. Looks like a missing tooth against the sky. Head for that
The old watchtower foundation is a good reference point. Four walls, knee-high, square. You can see it from a distance
Where the rock changes from grey to black. Volcanic, probably. The transition is sharp
The trees thin out before you reach it. Not cleared: they just don't grow well there. The soil might be wrong
A natural arch of stone spans the gap. You can walk under it. The path continues on the other side
The place reeks of rotting vegetation. Stagnant water, leaf mold, something decaying. Not dangerous, just unpleasant
Upstream from the waterfall. Not far: the sound covers conversation at about a hundred paces
The ground is littered with loose scree. Unstable footing. Move carefully or you'll slide
There's a seam of quartz running through the exposed rock face. White, glinting in sunlight. Follow it east
The old road is paved with fitted stones in one section. Overgrown now, but you can feel the flat surface under the weeds
A cluster of boulders that looks like a deliberate arrangement. Whether it is or isn't, it's a useful landmark
The area smells of pine resin. Strong, fresh. The trees there are particularly dense and the air is thick with it
You'll find a series of stone steps cut into the hillside. Old, worn, shallow. Someone went to real effort
Where the river bends sharply. The outside bank is undercut and steep. The inside bank is shallow gravel
There's a clearing with a single massive tree at its center. The tree is older than anything else in the area
The ground there is covered in lichen. Grey-green, crusty, thick. You're walking on decades of growth
Past the landslide scar. You can see where the hillside gave way: bare earth, exposed roots, jumbled stone
The entrance is at the base of a cliff face. Partially hidden by brush, but you can feel the cool air flowing out
A grove of willows marks the spot. They cluster around a seep spring and create a curtained enclosure
The stone walls of the old enclosure are still standing in places. Waist-high, dry-stacked, covered in moss
You'll hear the bats before you see the cave. At dusk, the sound of them emerging is audible from a distance
There's a ford in the river where the water barely reaches your knees. Downstream it deepens fast
The ground is sandy there. Not soil: actual sand, like a beach, but inland. The drainage must carry it
Along the base of the escarpment. The rock wall runs for a good stretch. The features you want are near the eastern end
The trail is marked with old blazes. Axe cuts in the bark, healed over but still visible. Someone marked this path a long time ago
There's a depression where water collects after rain. Not quite a pond: a temporary pool. Good place to find tracks
Where the treeline meets the grassland. That transition zone is narrow and distinctive
A section of exposed bedrock forms a natural platform. Flat, broad, slightly elevated. Good for surveying the surroundings
There's a tangle of fallen timber from a past storm. The deadfall creates a natural barricade across the path
The air changes temperature sharply at one point. Cold draft from underground. A passage opens nearby, even if you can't see it
Look for the lichen-covered outcrop that resembles a face in profile. Not artistic: just a coincidence of erosion
The canyon walls are close enough to touch both sides at once. Narrow, deep, shaded even at midday
A spring emerges from the base of a rock formation. Clear, cold water pooling in a natural basin
The meadow is flat and open. Exposed from every direction. You'll see anything approaching long before it arrives
Where the old foundation walls make a rectangular outline in the grass. Knee-high stone, overgrown but unmistakable
There's a stretch of path where the tree roots have been exposed. Knotted, thick, crossing the trail like rungs
The drainage channel runs straight for about two hundred paces before curving. Man-made, not natural
A patch of brambles so dense nothing gets through without being cut. The thorns are a hand-length long
The sinkhole is wider than it looks from the edge. The sides are steep and the bottom is obscured by debris
Near the confluence of two small streams. The water is deeper there and the banks are muddy
The ridge trail offers no shelter from the wind. It blows constantly up there, strong and cold
A row of cypress trees, evenly spaced, lines the old boundary. Planted deliberately, not wild growth
There's a section of cliff where the rock is fractured into columns. Natural formation, but it looks constructed
The fog collects in that hollow every morning. Burns off by midday, but early hours the visibility is nearly zero
An old stone wall runs along the contour of the hill. Not a building: a terrace wall, for farming on the slope
The waterfall drops into a pool deep enough to swim in. The spray keeps everything within twenty paces damp
Where the ground is covered in flat shale that clinks underfoot. Walk quietly there and you'll announce yourself
The tunnel entrance is screened by a curtain of roots from the tree growing above it
A natural amphitheater of stone, curved and enclosed on three sides. Sound carries strangely there
The trail descends steeply through a series of switchbacks. The turns are tight and the drop is considerable
Near the old boundary ditch. It's silted up now, but the line is still visible as a shallow depression
The marsh begins gradually. Solid ground gets spongy, then wet, then you're ankle-deep before you realize it
There's a stretch where the path runs along a ledge. Narrow, exposed, a drop on one side. Keep to the wall
A collapsed structure has left a mound of rubble covered in ivy. The outline suggests something larger than a house
The clearing has a fire ring that's been used many times. Blackened stones, ash layers, scattered charcoal
Where the bedrock is exposed in slabs. Smooth, tilted, water runs across them in thin sheets after rain
The area is dense with ferns. Waist-high in places. You can't see the ground underfoot
An abandoned quarry cut into the hillside. The walls are sheer, the floor is flat, and water pools at the lowest point
There's a gap in the ridge that funnels the wind. Walking through it, the gust can nearly knock you off your feet
The old road is discernible only by the absence of trees. A corridor of younger growth where the pavement used to be
Near a group of standing stones. Whether they were placed or are natural is debated, but they make a good meeting point
The slope is covered in loose shale that shifts underfoot. Two steps forward, one step sliding back
There's a grove where the trees are all the same age. Regrowth after a fire, probably. Uniform, dense, hard to move through
A gulch runs perpendicular to the main path. Dry most of the year, but when it rains it becomes a torrent
The cliff face has a series of ledges like rough stairs. Not carved: eroded. But usable, if you're careful
Where the underground stream surfaces briefly before disappearing again. A stretch of maybe ten paces where you can see the water
There's a smell of damp earth and minerals. Distinctive. You'd recognize it once you've been there
The ground is cratered with old dig sites. Shallow pits, long abandoned. Someone was looking for something
A bluff overlooks the junction. Good sightlines in three directions. The approach is visible from up there
The area is marked by unusually large anthills. Mounds taller than a child, built from red soil
Where the river cuts through a band of white limestone. The water there is clear to the bottom
The remnants of an old orchard. Trees still produce, though nobody tends them. The fruit is small and sour
A bench of rock runs along the hillside like a natural terrace. Level, wide enough to walk comfortably
There's a thicket of thorny shrubs that screens the approach. You'll need to go around unless you want to bleed
Where the terrain flattens unexpectedly. After a long climb, the plateau surprises you
The cave has multiple openings. The main entrance is the largest, but there are smaller ones higher on the rock face
Near where the old signal fire was maintained. The platform is still there, blackened and bare
The area is rich with wildflowers in the warm season. Even out of bloom, the stalks are distinctive
A shelf of rock overhangs the path. You duck under it without thinking. The clearance is just enough
There's a section of the trail that's been reinforced with log steps. Old, mossy, but still functional
Where the abandoned mine entrance has been boarded shut. The boards are weathered. Nobody's maintained the seal
The hollow is fed by a seasonal stream. In the dry months, the streambed is just a path of smooth stones
A tor rises from the ridge. A pillar of stone, taller than three men, visible from the valley below
Near the peat bog. The ground there is dark, spongy, and smells of earth. Don't step off the firm path
The place is marked by a distinct echo. Clap your hands and the sound comes back twice, sharp and clear
Where the stone is stained dark by mineral seepage. Black streaks down the rock face, wet and glistening
The old kiln site is recognizable by the brick fragments scattered in the soil. Nothing else grows well there
There's a bend in the trail where the view suddenly opens up. After walking through dense cover, the panorama is striking
A talus slope of angular rock fragments. Unstable, noisy, impossible to cross quietly
Near the sheltered cove where the boats were pulled up. The drag marks are still visible in the shingle
The corridor of stone narrows to a point where you must turn sideways to pass. Beyond it, the space opens
Where a tributary joins the main channel. The water churns at the confluence, muddy and turbulent
The rock there is flint. Strike a blade against it and you'll get sparks. The old toolmakers knew this place
A depression surrounded by raised ground on all sides. A natural bowl that collects water and wind equally
There's a line of erosion cuts in the hillside. Deep V-shaped gullies running parallel, like claw marks
The area is notable for its silence. No birds, no insects, no wind noise. Unsettling when you first notice it
Where an old fence line ran. The posts are gone but the ground remembers: a faint ridge in the grass
The pool at the base of the falls is surrounded by smooth, water-polished stone. Slippery when wet
A solitary oak stands in the middle of cleared ground. The trunk is wider than two people can reach around
The bank is undercut and overhanging. The earth above is held together by roots. It won't last many more seasons
Where the road was washed out. A gully now crosses the path where solid ground used to be
The area around the spring is lush. Green, overgrown, almost tropical in comparison to the surrounding terrain
There's a section of wall that's been rebuilt at least twice. You can see the different stone and mortar layers
A chimney of rock rises from the cave floor to the surface. Daylight filters down through it
The track follows the ridgeline for about a mile. Exposed, windswept, with views on both sides
Where a grove of ancient trees creates a canopy so dense that the ground beneath is bare. No light reaches the floor
A line of white-barked trees marks the edge of the wet ground. Beyond them, the footing changes
There's a cairn at the summit. Small, stacked carefully, with a flat stone on top. Someone's marker
The gulch is floored with river gravel. Smooth, round stones that shift and roll when you walk on them
Near where the aqueduct used to run. The channel is cut into the hillside, dry now, but the engineering is impressive
The cave system branches. Left goes deeper. Right loops back to the surface. Most people take the right
A ledge at about head height runs along the interior wall. Natural shelf, used for storage by whoever was here before
The area is thick with cobwebs. Old ones, undisturbed. Nothing has passed through this way in a long time
Where the path emerges from the tree line onto open ground. The transition is abrupt: cover to exposure in three steps
The field is scattered with glacial erratics. Boulders that don't match the local stone, deposited by ice long ago
There's a ledge trail cut into the cliff face. Narrow, but manageable. Don't look down unless you need to
Near the salt lick. The ground is worn bare by animals. A natural gathering point for wildlife
The ruin's doorway still stands. Lintel intact, jambs upright. Everything else has fallen away around it
A stretch of the river is shallow enough to wade across. Knee-deep, gravel bottom, moderate current
The soil there is chalky white. Alkaline, probably. Different from everything around it
Where the timber bridge used to span the ravine. The abutments are still standing on both sides. The span is gone
An opening in the rock face, roughly rectangular, leads to a passage that curves out of sight
There's a terrace of land below the main cliff that's sheltered from above. Protected ground, hard to see from the top
The path is lined with flat-topped stones that might once have been a proper walkway. Overgrown, but the intention is clear
Near the old charcoal-burning site. The ground is still black. Trees have not reclaimed it
A seasonal waterfall runs down the rock face in the wet months. In dry weather, the streak of discoloration marks where it flows
The valley floor is marshy in the center and firm at the edges. Stay to the sides if you don't want wet feet
Where the sun hits the cliff at a certain angle, the mineral deposits glow orange. Brief, beautiful, and useful for timing your arrival
There's a tunnel that passes completely through a ridge. Short enough to see light at both ends. A natural passage
An old well marks the site. Dry now, but the stonework is solid and the depth suggests it once served a significant population
The meadow slopes gently toward the river. Easy walking, open ground, no obstacles
Where the rock is honeycombed with small cavities. Wind blows through them and creates a low whistling sound
A platform of dry-stacked stone, roughly circular, about waist height. Someone built it for a purpose that's been forgotten
The area is marked by a distinct change in vegetation. On one side of an invisible line, one kind of plant. On the other, something else entirely
There's a cascade of small waterfalls, each dropping into a pool that feeds the next. The sound is constant and soothing
The approach is through a narrow defile. High walls on both sides. A natural chokepoint
Where an old bridge abutment juts from the bank. The stone is still solid, though the bridge is long gone
The ground is covered in a thick carpet of pine needles. Soft underfoot, quiet to walk on
Near the bend in the road where the milestone stands. The numbers are worn but still legible
A field of tall grass, shoulder height. The trail through it is barely visible. Follow the flattened stems
There's a shelter built into the rock face. Natural overhang, improved with a low wall at the front
Where the landscape is dotted with thermal vents. Wisps of steam rising from cracks in the ground
The cave has a chimney effect. Air flows through it constantly. Cool in summer, warm in winter
Near the old stone circle. The purpose is debated but the location is unmistakable
A long, narrow lake fills a valley between two ridges. The water is deep and dark. No shallow end
There's a natural causeway of packed earth across the marsh. Narrow, winding, but it keeps your feet dry
The hillside is terraced with old agricultural walls. Abandoned, but the structure persists
Where a massive root system from a fallen tree has lifted a wall of earth. The exposed hollow beneath is large enough to shelter in
The bluff drops sheer on three sides. The only approach is from the southwest, up a gradual slope
Near the junction where three tracks meet. A good reference point because each path leads somewhere different
The gorge walls are carved with channels from centuries of water flow. The patterns are deep and intricate
There's a flat area of compacted earth that suggests a threshing floor or gathering space. Circular, level, deliberate
A line of stakes driven into the riverbed marks a crossing point. The current is manageable between them
Where the wind has sculpted the sandstone into pillars and arches. Eerie shapes that look different from every angle
The path ends at a cliff face. Look left: there's a narrow ledge that continues along the rock wall
Near the grove where the bark of every tree has been rubbed smooth. Animals use it. Probably have for generations
The beach is made of rounded pebbles, not sand. Each wave pulls them back with a distinctive rattling sound
There's a chimney of stacked rock that serves as a landmark. Visible from a good distance in flat terrain
The area smells of clay and damp stone. Earthy, mineral, cool. Underground water is close to the surface
Where a rockslide has exposed a cross-section of the hillside. Layers of different stone, stacked like a cut cake
The approach from the north is easier. The south side is steep and exposed
An old hearth, cold for years, sits at the center of the ruin. The chimney still stands above the collapsed walls
The ground is covered in shattered pottery. Thin-walled, fine quality. Something was stored here and fell when the roof gave way
Past the grove of twisted oaks. The trunks lean toward the coast from decades of wind. Nothing grows straight there
There is a ledge about twenty feet up where birds nest in the hundreds. The noise and the droppings mark the place from a quarter mile away
A row of post holes in the ground is all that remains of a fence or palisade. The spacing is regular and deliberate. Whatever was enclosed is gone
The path crosses a stretch of exposed iron ore. The ground crunches underfoot and stains your boots a rusty orange
Where the creek goes underground, the bank is hollowed out in a smooth curve. The water spirals into the gap like a drain
There is a shelf of rock jutting from the cliff about halfway up that is covered in bird lime and feathers. The ledge trail passes directly beneath it
The valley narrows to a bottleneck where two rock walls almost touch. The passage is wide enough for one person. The acoustics make every sound carry
Near the old lime kiln. The walls are blackened inside and the ground around it is chalky white. The contrast makes it visible from the ridge
A cluster of birch trees grows in a perfect circle. No gaps, no strays. The roots probably share a common ancestor, but the symmetry is unnerving
The terrain drops suddenly where the old river channel cut through. A ten-foot drop, almost vertical, overgrown with ferns. The path skirts the edge
There is a section of cave wall that is covered in scratch marks. Not tool marks: something lived here and sharpened its claws on the stone
An old dock extends into the lake, half submerged. The pilings are still solid but the decking has rotted through. Someone maintained it once
Where the trail crosses a field of broken basalt columns. The hexagonal shapes look man-made but they are entirely natural. You walk across the tops of them like stepping stones
The hillside has a concave section that collects sound. Stand at the bottom and you can hear a whisper from the ridge. The shepherds use it to communicate
Near the old smokehouse foundation. The stone walls are intact to chest height and the interior still smells faintly of ash and cured meat
A wide, shallow cave at the base of the bluff serves as a natural waypoint. The ceiling is stained black from centuries of campfires. Travelers have been stopping here for a long time
The river splits around an island of packed gravel. Both channels are shallow enough to wade. The island itself is bare rock with a single wind-bent pine
There is a section of the path where the soil is a deep, vivid red. Iron-rich clay, exposed by erosion. It stains everything it touches
Where the old boundary wall meets the river, the stones have been incorporated into a natural dam. The pool behind it is deep and cold
A line of boulders runs across the meadow at irregular intervals. Too large to move, too regular to be coincidental. Some past event deposited them
The pass is narrow enough that you can see both valleys at once. The wind funnels through and carries the smell of whatever is growing on the other side
There is a section of cave where the ceiling is low enough to force you to your hands and knees. About thirty paces of crawling before it opens up again
Near the old trestle bridge supports. The timber has gone grey and splintered but the foundation stones are squared and level. Good engineering, wasted
The riverbank has been undercut so deeply that the tree roots dangle in the air above the water. One good flood will take the trees and several paces of bank with them
A seam of chalky white stone runs through the dark granite like a vein. The local name for the formation is the Scar. It is visible from three ridges away
Where the dry creek bed meets the main trail, there is a pile of smooth stones that travelers have stacked over the years. A tradition without a clear origin
The ground beneath the elder trees is always dry, even after heavy rain. The canopy is so dense and the leaf litter so thick that moisture simply does not reach the soil
There is a stretch of riverbed where the current has polished the stones to a mirror finish. At the right angle, the reflection of the sky makes the water disappear
Near the stone that has been split in two by a root. The halves sit a handspan apart and the root passes between them. The tree that owns the root is forty paces away
A depression in the rock holds rainwater year-round. Deep enough to drink from, never more than ankle-deep. The edges are smoothed by generations of animals that have come to it
The approach through the birch stand is deceptive. The trees are spaced far enough to walk through but the ground undulates in hidden dips that catch your feet
Where the old cart track crosses the stream, the stones laid as a ford are still in place. Flat, fitted, level. The water flows over them in a smooth sheet
There is a point on the ridgeline where you can see the settlement in one direction and open wilderness in the other. The contrast is sharper than you expect
The ground near the hot spring is warm enough to melt frost in winter. The grass grows taller and greener there than anywhere else. A patch of summer in a cold landscape
Near the collapsed section of the old wall. The rubble field extends about twenty paces and the individual stones are too large to step over. You climb or you go around
A shelf of limestone protrudes from the slope at about chest height. Flat, broad, and worn smooth by use. Everyone who walks this path stops to rest there. You can tell by the boot scuffs
The old quarry face is sheer and the exposed layers of stone are striped in greys and browns. Water seeps down the face in a thin film that catches the light
Where the trail descends through a stand of old-growth conifers, the ground is bare except for a thick blanket of needles. No undergrowth. No sound. Just the trees and the filtered light
There is a boulder perched on the edge of the ravine that looks like it should have fallen long ago. The locals call it the Waiting Stone. Nobody walks directly beneath it
The cave entrance is marked by a draft of cold air that you can feel from several paces away. In summer, the temperature difference is striking
Near the bend in the river where a gravel bar has accumulated. The bar shifts position after every flood but it always reforms in roughly the same spot
A stretch of the old paved road surfaces between two fields. About fifty paces of fitted stone, smooth and level, flanked by weeds. The rest of the road is buried
The terrain beyond the tree line is alpine: short grass, exposed rock, and a wind that never stops. The view compensates for the discomfort
Where a fallen pine has bridged a gap between two boulders, creating a natural arch. The bark has worn smooth from people using it as a crossing
There is a small cave behind the waterfall. Not deep, but dry. The curtain of water screens the entrance and the sound fills the space
Near the old stone pen where livestock was once held during storms. The walls are intact and the gate posts still stand. The pen would hold a dozen animals
The escarpment runs northwest for about half a mile, gaining height gradually. The highest point offers a vantage over the entire watershed
Where the old timber road enters the forest, the ruts are still visible as parallel depressions in the moss. The forest is reclaiming the route one season at a time
The approach to {subject_focus_the} is straightforward once you know the landmarks
The terrain around {subject_focus_the} is rougher than the maps suggest. Allow extra time
There is a good vantage point overlooking {subject_focus_the} from the south ridge
The path to {subject_focus_the} forks about halfway. Take the lower branch
Most people approaching {subject_focus_the} come from the east. The western approach is steeper but faster
There is a clearing near {subject_focus_the} that makes a reasonable staging area
Water is available about a quarter mile from {subject_focus_the}. A spring, reliable even in dry months
The area around {subject_focus_the} is exposed. Limited cover if things go sideways
The entrance to {subject_focus_the} faces downhill. You approach from above, which gives you the advantage
{subject_focus_The} is visible from the main road if you know where to look. Most people walk right past it
The footing near {subject_focus_the} is treacherous. Loose stone and wet soil in equal measure
The route to {subject_focus_the} passes through dense undergrowth. Bring something to clear a path
There is only one way in and out of {subject_focus_the}. That simplifies things and complicates them
The elevation at {subject_focus_the} is higher than expected. The climb is steady but relentless
{subject_focus_The} sits in a natural depression. Good for shelter, bad for drainage
The ridgeline above {subject_focus_the} offers a clear line of sight in every direction
The stone near {subject_focus_the} is dark and crumbling. Watch your footing on the approach
A dry streambed runs along the base of {subject_focus_the}. Useful as a path when the water is low
The wind at {subject_focus_the} funnels through a gap and can knock you off balance if you are not braced
There are remains of an old camp near {subject_focus_the}. Someone was there before you
The walls of {subject_focus_the} are intact on three sides. The fourth has collapsed inward
A ledge near {subject_focus_the} provides shelter from rain but not from wind
The canopy thins near {subject_focus_the}, so you lose tree cover for the last stretch
There is a distinctive rock formation near {subject_focus_the} that serves as a reliable landmark
The soil near {subject_focus_the} is loose and shifts underfoot. Stay to the rocky patches
{subject_focus_The} is easier to find in daylight. At night, the landmarks are unreliable
A fallen tree spans the ravine near {subject_focus_the}. Passable, but barely
The drainage from {subject_focus_the} feeds a marshy area to the south. Expect mud
There is a natural overhang near {subject_focus_the} that would serve as a fallback point
The approach to {subject_focus_the} narrows considerably in the last hundred paces. Single file only
